channel.title: NYT > Movies
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ Review: Lost in the Expansion
item.description: Born from a single unsettling image, this viral horror concept from Kane Parsons wears thin under the weight of a full feature.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Power Ballad’ Review: Hitting All the Wrong Notes
item.description: There are some promising themes in this comedy-drama about an American musician in Dublin, featuring Nick Jonas and Paul Rudd, but the movie never develops them.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Pressure’ Review: The Weather of War
item.description: Amid the chaos of World War II, this dull film centers on dueling weather reports in the period leading up to D-Day.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: Three Great Documentaries to Stream
item.description: This month’s picks explore the French legal system, a neighborhood conflict in Florida and the cinema legacy of the Lumière brothers.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg
item.media:description: In “12 Days,” the director Raymond Depardon every so often folds in exterior images among the scenes of judges interviewing psychiatric patients.

item.title: ‘The Breadwinner’ Review: Nate Bargatze, Man of the House
item.description: The comedian makes a big swing with this movie about a stay-at-home dad, and gets a big assist from three funny kids.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘Miss You, Love You’ Review: A Son Outsources His Solace
item.description: The mourning process feels theatrical in this dramedy starring Allison Janney as a new widow estranged from her son.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: The Cast of ‘I Love Boosters’ Want You to Think Critically
item.description: Four cast members from Boots Riley’s working-class comedy discuss capitalism, art, protest and trying to change the world (with some laughs thrown in).
item.dc:creator: Sandra E. Garcia and Dana Scruggs
item.media:credit: Dana Scruggs for The New York Times

item.title: How Cannes Is Grappling With Changes
item.description: Reporting from the Cannes Film Festival, our film critic Alissa Wilkinson describes how the event is both fending off and embracing aspects of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson and Edward Vega

item.title: Blake Lively’s Last Chance: A U.S. Judge’s Take on an Untested State Law
item.description: After the settlement with Justin Baldoni’s company, one potentially thorny legal issue remains: whether she is due damages under a new California law.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: Blake Lively settled her retaliation claims that stemmed from the making of the 2024 movie “It Ends with Us,” but she is continuing to seek compensation through another legal avenue.

item.title: ‘With Hasan in Gaza’ Review: An Elusive Search
item.description: The Palestinian filmmaker Kamal Aljafari has constructed this elliptical documentary out of material filmed in Gaza in 2001, when he was searching for a friend.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Propeller One-Way Night Coach’: John Travolta’s Directing Debut
item.description: John Travolta makes his directing debut with this short and sentimental coming-of-age film about a son’s first plane trip taken with his single mother.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘The Last Viking’ Review: Imagine Mads Mikkelsen
item.description: Mikkelsen plays a man convinced he’s John Lennon in a darkly comic crime film from the Danish auteur Anders Thomas Jensen.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: ‘Forastera’ Review: An Exquisitely Deconstructed Ghost Story
item.description: A teenager affects aspects of her late grandmother in this beguiling first feature set on the island of Mallorca.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘The Currents’ Review: Lost and Found
item.description: Milagros Mumenthaler’s superb, sensuously realized film follows an Argentine fashion stylist trying to overcome a deep sense of destabilization.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: The Real Story Behind “Pressure,” the Film About the D-Day Weather Forecast
item.description: The new film “Pressure” tells the story of the fateful D-Day weather forecast. Here’s what it got right and wrong from the historical record.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar

item.title: The ‘Becky Shaw’ Star Who ‘Does Horrible Things’ Oh So Well
item.description: The Tony nominee Alden Ehrenreich has been making audiences cry at this Broadway comedy in which he portrays a cynical money manager.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Brian Karlsson for The New York Times

item.title: Julia Louis-Dreyfus to Star in ‘Other Desert Cities’ on Broadway
item.description: The revival, also featuring Ed Harris, Allison Janney, Lily Rabe and Joe Keery, will begin performances in September at the Hudson Theater.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Louis-Dreyfus, photographed earlier this month at the Cannes film festival, will be making her Broadway debut in the play.

item.title: ‘Armageddon’: The Blockbuster Melodrama That Left Our Heads Spinning
item.description: Michael Bay wasn’t remotely worried about the science in his 1998 science-fiction action hit that launched an elite ensemble cast to space.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: Twenty Years After His Film, Al Gore Tweaks the Climate Script
item.description: Mr. Gore is still giving the slide show that “An Inconvenient Truth” was built around, but with changes that reflect a shift in the discussion of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan

item.title: Broadway’s ‘The Lost Boys’ and Its Flying Vampire Spectacle
item.description: The Broadway production of “The Lost Boys” takes full advantage of the Palace Theater’s abundant height and depth, making the show feel like “a massive trust fall.”
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and Sara Krulwich
item.media:description: Multiple actors take flight at the same time in the spectacle-heavy production, which requires a lot of backstage work to ensure nothing, and no one, collides.

item.title: Alamo Drafthouse Instituted Mobile Ordering. Super Fans Are Not Pleased.
item.description: Known for its strict no-phones policy, the Alamo Drafthouse has instituted mobile ordering for its in-seat meals, upsetting some of its biggest fans.
item.dc:creator: Brendan Klinkenberg
item.media:description: Moviegoers at an Alamo Drafthouse in Austin, Texas. It has long served food to patrons at their seats, but now they order by phone instead of writing out their requests.

item.title: How This Summer’s Movies Shift the Connection With Their Related Toys
item.description: “The Mandalorian and Grogu,” “Masters of the Universe” and “Toy Story 5” hail from franchises that are shifting the connection with related products.
item.dc:creator: Robert Ito
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
item.media:description: A fan carrying a Grogu doll, one of many products being used to market “Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu.”

item.title: How Marilyn Monroe Gave a Smart Gloss to Her Image
item.description: It was all about self-improvement for the actress, who was born a century ago next week. Two new volumes shed light on the books she collected and the intellectual she married.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: Marilyn and Her Books: The Literary Life of Marilyn Monroe (Book)
The Arthur Miller Tapes: A Life in His Own Words (Book)

item.title: Director Cristian Mungiu’s ‘Fjord’ Wins Palme d’Or at Cannes Film Festival
item.description: The film, from the Romanian director Cristian Mungiu, explores the tension between religious conservatism and social liberalism. The award closed a festival that felt comparatively muted to many.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis
item.media:description: The director Cristian Mungiu, center, accepted the Palme d’Or in Cannes, France, on Saturday.

item.title: As Draco Malfoy on Broadway, Tom Felton Draws a Crowd
item.description: Fans are traveling great distances for the chance to meet Tom Felton, who has revived a now grown-up Draco Malfoy on Broadway in “Harry Potter and The Cursed Child.”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr and Vidhya Nagarajan
item.category: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child (Play)

item.title: Shooting Stars at the amfAR Cannes Gala
item.description: Our photographer took in the art and fashion on display as guests gathered in seaside Antibes, France, to raise money for AIDS research.
item.dc:creator: Landon Nordeman
item.category: amfAR, The Foundation for AIDS Research
item.media:credit: Landon Nordeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Coco Rocha at the amfAR Cannes gala in Cap d’Antibes, France, on Thursday.

item.title: Which Cannes Films Could Become Oscar Contenders?
item.description: The American “Club Kid” and Spanish “La Bola Negra” are among the most likely to impress academy voters, while Scarlett Johansson and Javier Bardem also stood out.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:description: Jordan Firstman, center, in his directorial debut, “Club Kid,” which sold to A24 for a reported $17 million.

item.title: This Is How You Steal a Scene Using Only a Bank
item.description: Deposit slips, vintage ledgers and more: David Korins’s Tony-nominated, 27,000-pound set for “Dog Day Afternoon” is as much a technical feat as a 1970s throwback.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik and Amir Hamja
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times

item.title: Boots Riley Takes on Fast Fashion in a Surreal Comedy
item.description: Alissa Wilkinson, a New York Times film critic, reviews the very political “I Love Boosters.”
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Haimy Assefa, Edward Vega and Laura Salaberry

item.title: Five Horror Movies to Stream Now
item.description: Evil this month looks just plain weird, from a despairing children’s TV show to a demonic buffet.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.category: The House Was Not Hungry Then (Movie)

item.title: James Gray Stands Out at a Disappointing Cannes Film Festival
item.description: James Gray’s “Paper Tiger” is among the stronger titles, which also include Ryusuke Hamaguchi’s “All of a Sudden” and Pawel Pawlikowski’s “Fatherland.”
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis
item.media:description: Adam Driver is among the stars of “Paper Tiger,” directed by James Gray.

item.title: Billy Eichner on ‘Bros’ and His New Memoir
item.description: The comedian needed time to process the poor turnout for his gay rom-com, “Bros.” His vulnerable new memoir may surprise “Billy on the Street” fans.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.media:credit: Brian Karlsson for The New York Times

item.title: 7 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
Tom Clancy's Jack Ryan: Ghost War (Movie)

item.title: Watch Scorsese Make a Mean Sandwich in ‘The Mandalorian and Grogu’
item.description: The director Jon Favreau narrates a sequence featuring a character who works at a food truck and is voiced by Martin Scorsese.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)

item.title: ‘Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu’: What to Know Before Seeing the Movie
item.description: It draws from the Disney+ series. Here’s a reminder of the events of the show and how those fit into the larger franchise.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
item.media:description: Pedro Pascal as the Mandalorian and his little friend in a scene from the new movie.

item.title: ‘Girl, Interrupted’ Continues, This Time Onstage at the Public Theater
item.description: This 1993 memoir, which became a film with Winona Ryder and Angelina Jolie, is now a play with songs by Aimee Mann. Here’s how the latest iteration came to be.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: From left, Sally Shaw, Mia Pak, Juliana Canfield, Katherine Reis, King Princess and Ta’Rea Campbell in “Girl, Interrupted” at the Public Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: In ‘Ask E. Jean,’ a Trump Adversary Examines Her Decision to Speak Out
item.description: E. Jean Carroll won two court cases against him, but it took decades for her to come forward. The film is at its best on the reasons for her hesitance.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘The Mandalorian and Grogu’ | Anatomy of a Scene
item.description: The director Jon Favreau narrates a sequence from his film, featuring a character voiced by Martin Scorsese.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
